Consciousness Science Machine Minds Moral Status
Glossary About
Consciousness Science

Michael Tomasello

b. 1950

Shared intentionality and the social-developmental emergence of the human mind.

Michael Tomasello does not present a formal doctrine of the soul, nor does he offer a complete metaphysical theory of consciousness in the manner of Chalmers, Nagel, Searle, or Dennett. His work is primarily comparative psychology, developmental psychology, evolutionary anthropology, and social ontology. But it is philosophically rich enough to extract a highly coherent philosophy of mind from it. The core thesis is this: mind is not first of all an inner theater of private representations; mind is embodied, goal-directed agency that becomes distinctively human when it is transformed by shared intentionality into perspectival, linguistic, moral, and norm-governed participation in a common world. Consciousness, in this reconstructed Tomasellian view, is not just “what it is like” privately, but the experienced world of an agent; in humans, that experiential world is socially and normatively objectified. “Soul,” if the term is used responsibly, cannot mean in Tomasello an empirically discovered separable substance. It can, however, name the embodied, relational, morally accountable form of human personhood that his natural history describes and that theological interpreters may read as bearing spiritual significance.

1. Tomasello’s project and why it matters philosophically

Tomasello is best read as a theorist of the emergence of the human form of mind. He has worked across child development, great-ape cognition, communication, cooperation, culture, and morality; the Springer biographical entry describes him as a comparative and developmental psychologist whose work concerns the evolution of distinctively human cognition and behavior. His philosophical importance lies in the fact that he does not explain the human mind by beginning with language, abstract reason, symbolic thought, or Cartesian self-consciousness. He begins lower down: with intentional agency, cooperation, joint attention, shared goals, role-taking, recursive perspective-taking, and the internalization of social norms.

His central revision of his own earlier position is crucial. In The Cultural Origins of Human Cognition he had emphasized that humans understand others as intentional agents. Later ape data forced a refinement: great apes also understand goals and perceptions, but this does not yield language, institutions, or full human culture. The missing element is not intentionality as such, but the skills and motivations to share intentional states with others. In his own précis of A Natural History of Human Thinking, Tomasello explicitly says that the newer ape evidence showed that understanding others as intentional agents can be used competitively; only humans combine that understanding with the motivation and capacity to share intentional states.

This is why the 2005 article “Understanding and sharing intentions: The origins of cultural cognition” is foundational. There Tomasello and colleagues identify “shared intentionality” as the crucial difference between human cognition and that of other species: humans participate with others in collaborative activities involving shared goals and intentions, requiring intention-reading, cultural learning, motivation to share psychological states, and distinctive forms of cognitive representation. They connect this capacity to linguistic symbols, social norms, beliefs, and institutions.

The philosophical consequence is large: the human mind is not merely an upgraded primate mind with more memory or more intelligence. It is a mind restructured by a “we” relation. Tomasello later insists that shared intentionality is not an extra module added to an otherwise individual mind; it transforms cognitive representation, inference, and self-regulation.

2. The basic ontology: agency before “mind”

The most general concept in Tomasello’s mature framework is not consciousness, representation, or language. It is agency. In The Evolution of Agency, he describes organisms as feedback-control systems that pursue goals, make decisions, and monitor action. He proposes a sequence: goal-directed agency in ancient vertebrates, intentional agency in mammals, rational agency in great apes, and socially normative agency in ancient humans. Each stage increases complexity in planning, decision-making, executive control, and experience, culminating in the human experience of an objective and normative world.

This gives us the first major thesis of a Tomasellian philosophy of mind: mind is a form of organized agency. It is not an occult inner entity hidden behind behavior; but neither is it reducible to external behavior. Mind consists in the organism’s capacity to represent goal-relevant situations, infer possible outcomes, choose among actions, and monitor its own cognitive and practical processes. That makes Tomasello a naturalist, but not a crude behaviorist. His account requires representations, inferences, self-monitoring, and perspective-taking.

In A Natural History of Human Thinking, he defines thinking as an occurrent process involving three sets of cognitive processes: offline cognitive representations, inferential simulations based on causal, intentional, and logical relations, and cognitive self-monitoring. This triad is the backbone of his philosophy of mind. Representation gives the agent something to think about; inferential simulation lets the agent anticipate and reason; self-monitoring lets the agent evaluate its own cognition and action.

This is also where Tomasello differs from more language-first theories of mind. Language is enormously important for him, but it is not the first cause of human cognition. It is made possible by earlier shared intentional structures: joint attention, common ground, cooperative motivation, pointing, pantomime, and role-sensitive collaboration. Human linguistic consciousness grows out of cooperative agency; it does not drop into the world as an autonomous symbolic faculty.

3. Three layers of mind: individual, joint, and collective intentionality

Tomasello’s mature account is best organized around three levels: individual intentionality, joint intentionality, and collective intentionality. These are not merely social categories; they are different forms of mind.

Individual intentionality is characteristic of great apes. Tomasello argues that great apes do indeed think. They represent entities and situations schematically; they attend voluntarily to goal-relevant facts; they make causal and intentional inferences; and they monitor their own cognitive processes. Their representations are not mere sensory images. They are structured around action-relevant “situations” or “states of affairs,” such as whether food is reachable, whether another sees it, or whether an action will work.

This matters because Tomasello does not deny mind to nonhuman animals. On the contrary, he attributes to great apes a significant form of rational agency. In his 2022 article “What is it like to be a chimpanzee?” he argues that chimpanzee experience is intentional in a way basically similar to humans, rational in the sense of self-critical and inferential, but not normative in the sense of operating with objective evaluative standards. He also notes that scientific data do not settle philosophical questions about consciousness, but they provide material for clarifying psychological concepts.

Joint intentionality is the first distinctively human breakthrough. It is dyadic and second-personal: “I with you,” not yet “we as a whole culture.” In joint intentionality, partners form joint goals, divide roles, attend together to the same object or task, and understand each other’s perspectives on the shared focus. This generates a “shared world comprising distinct individual perspectives.” Tomasello treats pointing and pantomime as early forms of cooperative communication that require the communicator to intend that the recipient attend to something and infer its relevance.

The philosophical importance of joint intentionality is that it creates perspectival mind. The infant or early human does not merely see an object; she sees an object as jointly attended to by self and other, with each participant having a role and a perspective. This is the foundation of aspectuality, communicative reference, and later linguistic meaning. A thing can now be “the same thing” under different perspectives. That is a major step toward objectivity.

Collective intentionality is the later cultural form. It is not just “you and I doing something together,” but “we, as members of a group, do and judge things this way.” It introduces conventions, norms, institutions, cultural common ground, and the authoritative voice of the group. Tomasello says modern humans become group-minded creatures whose collective intentionality includes cultural conventions, norms, institutions, and linguistic symbols. The group’s perspective becomes objectified: what “anyone who would be one of us” must recognize. Internalizing this voice produces normative self-monitoring or self-governance.

So the human mind develops from action-oriented cognition to dyadic perspectival cognition to collectively norm-governed cognition. This is not a simple accumulation. It is a transformation of the architecture of mind.

4. Consciousness: the “experiential niche” rather than the Cartesian theater

Tomasello does not offer a complete theory of phenomenal consciousness. He does not solve the hard problem, and he does not claim to derive subjective experience from third-person data. His 2022 article on chimpanzee experience is explicit that scientific data do not answer philosophical questions by themselves. But he does give us a powerful way of thinking about consciousness comparatively: each kind of agent inhabits an experiential niche structured by its mode of agency.

For great apes, consciousness is intentional and rational but not normative. That is, chimpanzees experience the world as a world of objects, agents, goals, obstacles, competitors, opportunities, and causal relations. They can monitor their own actions and infer what others are likely to do. But they do not, on Tomasello’s account, experience the world under objective norms of correctness, obligation, justification, or cultural authority.

Human consciousness adds the experience of objectivity and normativity. In the agency framework, human beings come to experience not merely “what I want” or “what you see,” but “what is true,” “what we ought to do,” “what counts as correct,” “what anyone rational should recognize,” and “what one of us must do.” The human conscious field is therefore not just perceptual or affective; it is normative. The world shows up as binding, evaluable, and shareable.

This gives us a distinctive answer to the question “What is consciousness?” A Tomasellian answer would be: consciousness is the lived field of an agent’s practical orientation; in humans, that field is transformed by shared intentionality into an experienced world of common objects, perspectives, reasons, norms, and obligations. This is neither Cartesian dualism nor eliminative materialism. It is a genealogical and functional account of how different structures of agency yield different structures of experience.

5. Selfhood: from self-monitoring to accountability

The self in Tomasello is not a purely inward ego. It begins as agentive self-monitoring and becomes social self-monitoring, then normative self-governance.

Great apes self-monitor in the sense that they track what they are doing cognitively and practically. They know, in some operational sense, whether they have enough information, whether a strategy is working, or whether another agent has seen something. Tomasello is careful about philosophical vocabulary: when he speaks of ape “knowledge,” he does not mean justified true belief, but something like knowing that another has seen or registered an event.

In joint intentionality, self-monitoring becomes second-personal. I monitor myself from your point of view because my communicative act must work for you. When I point, gesture, or pantomime, I must simulate what you will attend to and infer. This is the birth of a socially mediated self.

In collective intentionality, self-monitoring becomes moral and cultural self-governance. I do not merely ask what you think of me; I ask what “we,” including myself as one of us, think of me. This is central to Tomasello’s account of morality: the individual becomes both judged and judge, internalizing shared standards. In his précis of A Natural History of Human Morality, he characterizes morality as a form of cooperation, a “we > me” orientation grounded in interdependence, and says human morality arises from psychological processes of shared intentionality.

This is one of the most important points for extracting a philosophy of mind: human self-consciousness is not merely reflective awareness. It is accountable selfhood. To be a human self is to stand under reasons, commitments, norms, and possible blame or respect. The self becomes a bearer of obligations.

6. Other minds, false belief, and objectivity

Tomasello’s theory of mind is deeply social. He does not think children simply infer hidden mental states in isolation, nor does he reduce mindreading to a built-in module. His 2018 PNAS article on false belief argues that young children come to understand false beliefs through social and communicative interactions that require them to compare perspectives. The key process is social and mental coordination with others; shared intentionality, joint attention, language, and executive function help children coordinate conflicting perspectives and distinguish subjective belief from objective reality.

The philosophical payoff is that objectivity is not given at the start. It emerges from perspectival coordination. In Tomasello’s response to commentators, he accepts that there is a serious philosophical difficulty: does joint intentionality already presuppose objective content? His answer is developmental and evolutionary rather than purely analytic. Great apes already have individual intentionality sufficient for some collaboration; under new selective pressures, collaborative practices could select for joint goals and shared intentionality. Dyadic perspective-taking then becomes a middle step toward the fully objective perspective of collective intentionality.

This means that objectivity is neither simply private correspondence to reality nor mere social convention. It is the stabilized outcome of coordinated perspectives, eventually generalized into the standpoint of “anyone who would be one of us.” This is close to a social-pragmatist theory of objectivity: the objective is what can be held in common across perspectives under shared standards of correction.

7. Language: not the source of mind, but the crystallization of shared mind

Tomasello’s philosophy of language follows from his philosophy of mind. Language is not an innate autonomous module that creates human sociality. It is the conventional and symbolic elaboration of a prior cooperative infrastructure. Human communication begins with helping and sharing: requesting help, informing others, sharing attention, sharing attitudes, and bonding around common objects and events.

The 2005 shared intentionality paper already links collaborative participation to linguistic symbols, norms, beliefs, and institutions. The later account of joint intentionality specifies the mechanism: pointing and pantomiming create an ostensive-inferential communicative structure in which one agent intends another to attend to something and infer its relevance. Common ground is required, because the recipient must know enough shared background to grasp why the gesture matters.

Language then amplifies the same dual-level structure: shared topic, individual perspective. A conversation is not merely sound exchange; it is joint attention to mental content. This explains why language is so central to false-belief understanding: conversation lets children coordinate perspectives on the same topic, including cases where one person’s representation diverges from reality.

The resulting philosophy of language is usage-based, pragmatic, cooperative, and social-ontological. Words are not merely labels glued to private ideas. They are conventional tools for coordinating attention, inference, role, perspective, and norm.

8. Morality: cooperation becomes normativity

Tomasello’s moral psychology is inseparable from his philosophy of mind. Morality is not an external layer placed on cognition after the fact. It emerges from the same shared-intentional architecture that makes human thinking human.

In the moral account, early humans became interdependent cooperative foragers. Interdependence made it rational and adaptive to care about partners, commitments, fair distribution, reliability, and reputation within the cooperative relation. The first moral form is second-personal: obligations to a partner with whom one has a joint commitment. Later, large-scale cultural life creates group-level norms, conventional standards, and objective morality.

This has several philosophical consequences. First, morality is not reducible to altruistic feeling. Sympathy matters, but fairness, obligation, guilt, shame, resentment, blame, responsibility, and respect also matter. Second, morality is not reducible to fear of punishment or reputation management. It is rooted in the internalized “we” standpoint. Third, morality is not originally religious in Tomasello’s genealogy. Proto-morality arises from cooperative interdependence before organized religion interprets, ritualizes, or sacralizes it.

This does not make morality unreal. On the contrary, Tomasello’s account explains why moral norms have legitimacy for the individual: the individual is not merely obeying an alien force, but participating in a shared form of life in which she is both judge and judged. Morality becomes the normative structure of human selfhood.

9. Religion and theology: not a distraction, but a serious extension

Tomasello himself has written comparatively little about religion, but when he does address it directly, the comments are significant. In his 2023 Routledge chapter “Some Thoughts on Evolution, Culture, and Religion,” he notes that evolutionary accounts of institutional religion often emphasize supernatural punishment, but he follows a Durkheimian suggestion that organized religions, at least in Western cases, characteristically emphasize the inspirational and aspirational aspects of cooperation: individuals are encouraged to emulate ideal cooperative types.

That is a theological opening. Religion, on this view, is not merely a coercive system for policing selfishness. It can be an institution of aspiration: saints, heroes, prophets, divine exemplars, rituals, and sacred narratives shape cooperative ideals. This connects religion to moral imagination and the formation of the self. Religious practices do not invent cooperation from nothing; they interpret, intensify, ritualize, and idealize the cooperative moral life that Tomasello’s anthropology describes.

Martin Breul’s 2025 article in Religions develops this point explicitly. Breul argues that Tomasello’s evolutionary anthropology is relevant to the mind–brain problem because it offers a non-reductive genealogical perspective: human consciousness is naturally evolved and brain-rooted, but it is not adequately understood by a simple monism-versus-dualism frame. The mind must be understood through its history of intersubjective and cooperative formation.

Breul also argues that Tomasello can support a Kantian “practical metaphysics.” Morality is not founded by religion; rather, morality is autonomous and historically prior to religion. But moral practice may rationally lead to theistic interpretation, including Kantian themes such as God and the immortality of the soul. Importantly, Breul stresses that Tomasello’s anthropology does not imply religious belief; it opens a space for theological reflection on the genealogy and validity of religious interpretations.

This is the right way to handle theology in relation to Tomasello. A crude debunking argument would say: if religion evolved from cooperation, it is false. Breul’s reply is that genealogy does not settle validity. Showing how religious meanings emerge from human cooperation does not prove God; but neither does it disprove God. It may instead show why religious interpretation is rooted in fundamental features of human moral and communal life.

10. The “soul”: what can and cannot be extracted

Tomasello gives no empirical proof of an immortal soul. His framework is evolutionary, developmental, and naturalistic. If “soul” means a separable immaterial substance detectable behind psychological life, Tomasello’s work neither establishes it nor requires it. A rigorous report should not pretend otherwise.

But “soul” has not always meant a Cartesian ghost. In broader philosophical and theological usage, it can mean the living form of a person, the depth of moral agency, the seat of relational selfhood, or the spiritual significance of embodied rational life. Under those meanings, Tomasello’s work is highly relevant. It gives a natural history of how a creature becomes a person: an embodied agent who shares attention, coordinates perspectives, gives and asks for reasons, enters commitments, internalizes norms, feels guilt and obligation, participates in institutions, and experiences the world as objectively and morally binding.

The strongest Tomasellian reconstruction would say: the human “soul,” if one uses that term, is not first a private interior substance but a relationally formed mode of agency. It is the human organism as transformed by shared intentionality into a participant in a common normative world. Its marks are not disembodied privacy, but joint attention, communicative rationality, moral accountability, and self-governance under shared norms.

This interpretation resonates with theological work on the imago Dei and imago Trinitatis. Robert Elliot’s 2023 article “Joint Attention and the Imago Trinitatis” argues that Tomasello, Carpenter, and colleagues have advanced a theologically significant hypothesis about human distinctiveness: humans share attention with conspecifics in a unique way, and this can illuminate the Christian doctrine that human beings are created in the image of the Trinity. A related theological proposal argues that Tomasello’s shared intentionality can bridge structural and relational accounts of the image of God: it is a capacity, but a capacity for relation.

This is the most promising theological appropriation. Tomasello does not give theology a proof of the soul. He gives theology a scientifically informed anthropology in which human distinctiveness is neither sheer rational abstraction nor mere biological continuity, but relational, cooperative, communicative, and normative personhood.

11. Non-English and non-Anglo reception

The non-Anglo reception is genuinely useful, because it often makes the philosophical stakes more explicit. German public-intellectual reception, for example, frames Tomasello’s project as an “evolution of spirit/mind” in which great apes possess individual intentionality but human thinking is oriented toward commonality, shared goals, and “Wir-Intentionalität.”

Portuguese-language philosophical reception is also direct. Viviane Zarembski Braga’s 2023 article “Do ‘Eu’ ao ‘Nós’” presents Tomasello’s account as a movement from the individual “I” to the shared “we,” emphasizing shared intentionality, mind-reading, metarepresentation, perspective-taking, objective thought, language, culture, morality, and higher sociability. Earlier Brazilian reception also focused on Tomasello’s model of cognitive-linguistic evolution and symbolic development, though some SciELO pages were not accessible through ordinary browsing.

Italian reception is particularly philosophically explicit. Treccani’s entry on “intenzionalità condivisa” says that Tomasello’s theory naturalizes mind in a Darwinian-evolutionary framework while integrating cultural aspects, and that it recalibrates philosophy of mind and cognitivism by placing social and cultural relations at the center rather than treating them as mere channels for transmitting representations. An Italian theological-relational ontology essay goes further, interpreting the triadic scene of child, adult, and pointed-to object as a space of communion that can be read in Trinitarian and spiritual terms.

French reception offers a useful contrast. Étienne Bimbenet’s article on Trần Đức Thảo and joint attention connects pointing with the origin of language and consciousness, arguing that modern joint-attention research supports the deep socialization of the human mind but may still remain too tied to an Anglo-cognitivist framework that treats the common as one dimension of mind rather than as constitutive of consciousness itself. This is a powerful critique to bring back to Tomasello: perhaps he still preserves too much individualism at the base, even while making shared intentionality transformative.

Spanish-language reception includes a 2023 chapter titled “Lo que nos hace humanos es la intencionalidad compartida. Michael Tomasello,” which signals that the Spanish philosophical-anthropological discussion has also centered on shared intentionality as the key to human distinctiveness, although the full text was not openly available in the source consulted.

The multilingual pattern is striking: English-language reception often debates empirical details and philosophy-of-action categories; German and Portuguese sources emphasize the movement from individual to communal mind; Italian and theological sources draw out relational ontology; French reception more readily presses the question of whether shared attention is merely a social capacity or the very origin of human consciousness.

12. Critical pressures and unresolved problems

The first major pressure is the emergence problem. Does joint intentionality explain objective content, or does it already presuppose it? Tomasello’s commentators raised this objection, and he acknowledges it as serious. His answer is not a deductive solution but an evolutionary-developmental dialectic: ape individual intentionality is enough for proto-collaboration; new cooperative pressures select for joint goals and shared attention; dyadic perspective-taking then becomes the bridge to collective objectivity. Whether this fully solves the problem remains debatable.

The second pressure is animal continuity. Tomasello’s account grants great apes much more cognition than older anthropocentric theories did, but he still draws a sharp line at objective normativity and shared intentionality. Recent work on animal normativity challenges simple claims that social norms are uniquely human, suggesting methods for studying “normative regularities” in nonhuman animals. This does not refute Tomasello, because “normative regularity” may be weaker than Tomasello’s objective, self-governed normativity. But it complicates any over-simple human exceptionalism.

The third pressure is consciousness. Tomasello gives an account of experiential niches, not a metaphysics of qualia. His approach can say much about how chimpanzee and human experience are structured by agency, rationality, and normativity, but it does not explain why there is subjective experience at all. That is a limitation if one asks for a full philosophy of consciousness in the hard-problem sense.

The fourth pressure is theology. Tomasello’s work is compatible with theological interpretation, but it does not force it. A theologian may read shared intentionality as a natural sign of relational personhood, imago Dei, or imago Trinitatis; a secular naturalist may read it as an evolved psychological adaptation. The empirical theory underdetermines the metaphysics. Breul’s caution is apt: Tomasello’s anthropology opens space for theological reflection; it does not entail religious belief.

The fifth pressure is cultural diversity. Tomasello’s model explains the general capacity for collective intentionality, but actual human cultures differ dramatically in norms, institutions, child-rearing practices, religious forms, and concepts of personhood. His framework needs supplementation from anthropology, history, and cross-cultural psychology to avoid turning a general developmental-evolutionary model into a uniform story of “the human mind.”

13. A reconstructed Tomasellian philosophy of mind

The system that can be extracted from Tomasello may be stated as follows.

First, mind is agency. The basic unit is not a detached representation but an organism pursuing goals, making decisions, and monitoring action.

Second, intentionality is practical before it is theoretical. Mental content begins as goal-relevant orientation toward situations and possible actions.

Third, representation is perspectivalized through cooperation. Great apes represent action-relevant situations; humans learn to represent the same situation from multiple coordinated perspectives.

Fourth, inference is simulation for action. Thinking involves imagining causal, intentional, and logical relations in order to decide what to do.

Fifth, selfhood begins as self-monitoring and culminates in normative self-governance. The human self is an accountable agent.

Sixth, other minds are not discovered by solitary speculation. They are understood through interaction, joint attention, communication, and perspective coordination.

Seventh, objectivity is socially achieved but not merely arbitrary. The objective world is the world stabilized across perspectives under shared norms of correction.

Eighth, language is conventionalized shared attention. It arises from cooperative communicative motives and then vastly expands the mind’s powers.

Ninth, morality is the normativity of interdependence. Human beings owe things to one another because their form of life is constituted by cooperation, commitment, and shared standards.

Tenth, consciousness is an experiential niche. Animal consciousness differs according to the structure of animal agency; human consciousness is distinctive because the world is experienced as shared, objective, and normatively binding.

Eleventh, culture is not an external environment added to mind. It is part of the architecture through which human cognition becomes objective, linguistic, institutional, and moral.

Twelfth, religion is a second-order interpretation and intensification of cooperative moral life. It is not the evolutionary origin of morality, but it can ritualize, idealize, and theologically interpret the moral world.

Thirteenth, the soul, if the term is retained, should be understood in this framework as embodied relational personhood: the living human agent as capable of shared attention, reason-giving, moral accountability, and participation in a normatively structured common world. This is not an empirical proof of immortality, but it is a serious natural history of what many traditions have meant by the depth of the human person.

14. Final assessment

Tomasello’s implicit philosophy of mind is one of the most important contemporary alternatives to both Cartesian individualism and reductive materialism. Against Cartesianism, he shows that the human mind is not built from solitary interiority outward; it is constituted through shared attention, cooperation, communication, and normativity. Against reductionism, he shows that explaining the human mind requires irreducibly social, developmental, and historical categories: joint goals, common ground, institutions, obligations, and cultural self-governance.

Theologically, his work is neither a proof nor a threat. It is a resource. It supports a relational anthropology in which the human person is not an isolated rational substance but a being whose very rationality is formed in communion. If theology speaks of the soul, Tomasello pushes it away from a disembodied ghost-model and toward a relational, moral, communicative, and embodied account of personhood. If philosophy speaks of consciousness, Tomasello pushes it away from a purely private theater and toward the lived, shared, normative world of agency.

The most comprehensive extraction, then, is this: the human mind is the cooperative becoming-objective of animal agency; human consciousness is the experience of a world opened by shared intentionality; and the human soul, in a philosophically and theologically disciplined sense, is the embodied person as participant in a common world of meaning, obligation, and transcendence-oriented interpretation.

Digital Minds · A history of thinking about the mind, from antiquity to the present day.
A personal research project by Fabián. · About · Glossary
